segments.text:
Milkweeds ( Asclepias spp. )
>130 species; greatest diversity in North America
Perennial plants; often with milky latex
Latex produced by milkweeds is toxic

Chemical defenses in milkweeds
Latex gums up insect mouthparts
Toxins at higher concentration in latex
Cardioglycosides (toxic to vertebrates)
Cysteine proteases (interfere with insect digestion)
Trichomes (hairs on leaves deter feeding)
Asclepias syriaca
Common milkweed
Indiana has 15 species of Asclepias

Interacting species
Pollinators - Transfer pollen from flower to flower; necessary for fruit production
Herbivores - Feed on leaves, flowers, nectar, and even the latex
Predators that eat herbivores
Pathogens, such as viruses and fungi.  Yes, plants also have viral pathogens!
The next pages show images of milkweed herbivores, and also of some additional species you might see on milkweeds during Lab 2.

Chewing insects
Monarch butterfly caterpillars
Milkweed tussock moth caterpillar
Red milkweed beetles
Milkweed leaf miners
Swamp milkweed leaf beetles
Milkweed stem weevil (mostly chews on stems)

Male has black spots on hind wing
Very young monarch caterpillar
Female has thicker black wing veins;
no spots on hind wing
First instar feeding (small holes located between laticifers )
Later instar feeding (cut laticifers to drain leaf of latex, can eat entire leaf)
Monarch butterfly ( Danaeus plexippus )
Monarch eggs

Caterpillars tend to feed in groups
milkweed tussock caterpillar
Milkweed tussock moth ( Euchaetes egle )

Has 4 eyes and long antennae
Antenna located between each pair of eyes
Tend to feed at leaf tip
Red milkweed beetle ( Tetraopes tetrophthalmus )

Liriomyza asclepiadis
This is a tiny fly larva
Eats soft tissues inside the leaf
The larvae live inside the leaf and eat leaf tissues.  This creates a pattern of skeletonized damage.
Leaf miners

Mottled tortoise beetle
Deloyala guttata 5-7 mm
Similar-looking: Lady beetle - “Ladybug” (adult & larva)
Eats insects, not milkweeds; 4-8 mm
Swamp milkweed leaf beetle
Labidomera clivicollis
Specialist on milkweed; 9-13 mm (big!)

Milkweed Stem Weevil ( Rhyssomatus lineaticollis)
Look for wilted leaves
Dried latex from weevil feeding

Sucking insects
Small milkweed bugs
Large milkweed bugs
Aphids

Small milkweed bugs ( Lygaeus kalmii )
adult
nymph
Nymph:
Abdomen all red in young nymphs, developing black spots with age;
Wingpads black, lengthening with age;
P ronotum red with 2 black diagonal markings
Adult:
Dark gray to black with broad orange or red band on forewing, forming an "X" shape that doesn't quite meet in the middle;
Head black with dull red spot on top, sometimes extending down onto face.
Piercing sucking insect that feeds gregariously in the nymphal stage, and is frequently found feeding on milkweed pods

Nymph:
Early instars have a bright orange abdomen, developing black spots with age
No black markings on pronotum
Adult:
Overall black and orange
Orange backward-pointing triangle posteriorly, separated by a black band in the middle
Freshly-molted young adults are very pale yellow, their color becoming darker and more orangish with age
adult
nymphs
Large milkweed bugs ( Oncopeltus fasciatus )
Piercing sucking insect that feeds on the seeds, leaves and stems of milkweed.  Found in small groups on milkweed.

Yellow oleander aphid ( Aphis nerii )
Can form large colonies on milkweeds
Native to Mediterranean, but introduced in North America
Drab-colored milkweed aphid ( Aphis asclepiadis )
Narrow specialist with more than 10 hosts (mostly Asclepias spp.)
Feeds in smaller colonies
Aphids
Aphids often tended by ants that collect sugary honeydew aphids excrete; in some cases protect aphids from predators.
Formica podzolica ants tending A. asclepiadis protect aphid colonies from lethal fungal infections caused by an obligate aphid pathogen, Pandora neoaphidis .

Other animals you might see
Slugs or snails
Ailanthus webworm moth
Praying mantis
White flies
Leaf-rolling caterpillars

Snails and slugs (these are not insects)
Look for slime trails on leaf
Lacy edge where feeding occurred
Slime trail
Lacy edge

Leaf roller caterpillars
Ailanthus webworm moth ( Atteva aurea )
Praying mantis - predator
